lesson.title: Sample Spaces & Events
lesson.body: The sample space Ω is the set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. An event is any subset A ⊆ Ω; it occurs when the observed outcome belongs to A. Set operations — complement Aᶜ, union A∪B, and intersection A∩B — let us build complex events from simpler ones. Two events A and B are mutually exclusive (disjoint) when A∩B = ∅, meaning they cannot both occur.

rooms.question: A fair six-sided die is rolled once. Which of the following correctly describes the sample space Ω?
steps: List every possible outcome of one die roll.
Each face value 1 through 6 is a distinct outcome.
rooms.explanation: Rolling one die yields exactly six outcomes: the integers 1 through 6. There is no face labeled 0 or 7, so the sample space is {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.

rooms.question: Let Ω = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} and A = {2, 4, 6}. What is the complement Aᶜ?
steps: The complement contains all outcomes in Ω that are NOT in A.
rooms.explanation: Aᶜ = Ω \ A = {1,2,3,4,5,6} \ {2,4,6} = {1,3,5}. The complement keeps every element of Ω that does not appear in A.

rooms.question: Let A = {1, 2, 3} and B = {3, 4, 5} be events in Ω = {1,2,3,4,5,6}. Find A ∪ B and A ∩ B.
steps: Union: include every element appearing in A or B (or both).
Intersection: include only elements appearing in both A and B.
rooms.explanation: A ∪ B collects {1,2,3} and {3,4,5} into {1,2,3,4,5}. A ∩ B is the overlap, which is just {3}.

rooms.question: Order these set-theoretic relationships from most restrictive (fewest elements guaranteed) to least restrictive (most elements guaranteed), for non-empty events A ⊆ Ω:
rooms.explanation: Disjoint events have no overlap (empty intersection). The intersection A∩B is a subset of A, so it has at most as many elements as A. A itself sits between its intersection and union with B. The union A∪B is always at least as large as either A or B alone.

rooms.question: Two events A and B satisfy A ∩ B = ∅. What does this imply?
steps: Recall the definition of mutually exclusive events.
If no outcome belongs to both A and B, they cannot co-occur.
answers: A and B are mutually exclusive; they cannot both occur.
A and B are independent events.
rooms.explanation: A ∩ B = ∅ means A and B share no outcomes, so they are mutually exclusive. Independence is a separate concept involving probabilities, not mere set overlap. Aᶜ = B would require A ∪ B = Ω as well, which isn't guaranteed here.

boss.name: The Null Set
boss.taunt: Your events collapse into ∅ — nothing you build will ever occur!

questions.question: Let A and B be events such that A ⊆ B. Which of the following must be true?
questions.explanation: When A ⊆ B, every element of A is also in B, so the intersection of A and B is exactly A. The union would be B, not A.

questions.question: Using De Morgan's law, which expression equals (A ∪ B)ᶜ?
questions.explanation: De Morgan's first law states (A ∪ B)ᶜ = Aᶜ ∩ Bᶜ. An outcome is outside the union precisely when it is outside both A and outside B.

questions.question: A coin is flipped twice. Let A = {HH, HT} (first flip is H) and B = {HT, TT} (second flip is T). What is A ∩ Bᶜ?
questions.explanation: Bᶜ = {HH, TH} (outcomes where the second flip is H). A ∩ Bᶜ = {HH, HT} ∩ {HH, TH} = {HH}.
